overview.top_countries.long_desc: This ranking compares 160 countries by Gross Domestic Product and key economic indicators. GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced — but the real story is in GDP per capita, which shows how that wealth translates to individual prosperity. Countries with high GDP per capita, low unemployment, and manageable debt tend to offer their citizens the best economic opportunities and quality of life.
overview.top_press.long_desc: Press freedom is the oxygen of democracy. This ranking uses the World Press Freedom Index to compare how freely journalists can work in 160 countries — from threats and censorship to media ownership concentration. Countries at the top protect independent journalism as a fundamental right. Those at the bottom silence critical voices through violence, imprisonment, or state-controlled media.
overview.top_satisfaction.long_desc: Life satisfaction goes beyond money — it measures how people actually feel about their lives. Based on the World Happiness Report, this ranking captures self-reported well-being across 160 countries. The happiest nations consistently combine strong social support, personal freedom, trust in institutions, and healthy life expectancy. GDP matters, but generosity and low corruption matter just as much.
overview.top_rule_of_law: Top Countries by Rule of Law
overview.top_rule_of_law.long_desc: The rule of law is what separates functioning societies from chaotic ones. This ranking measures whether laws apply equally to everyone — from citizens to presidents — using the World Justice Project Index and Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index. High-scoring countries have independent courts, low corruption, accessible justice, and governments that respect their own rules.

ranking.overall.long_desc: The World Barometer Overall Ranking scores 160 countries across 12 pillars of national prosperity — from governance and security to health, education, and innovation. Each pillar is scored 0–100 based on dozens of indicators from trusted international sources. The overall score is the average of all 12 pillars, giving a comprehensive picture of how well a country serves its people. No single number tells the whole story, but this ranking reveals clear patterns: countries that invest in institutions, education, and inclusion consistently outperform those that don't.
pillar.governance.long_desc: How well a country is governed shapes everything else. This pillar measures the strength of democratic institutions, the independence of courts, government transparency, corruption control, regulatory quality, and the effectiveness of public administration. Countries that score high have strong checks and balances, free press, low corruption, and citizens who trust their institutions.
pillar.security.long_desc: Safety is the foundation of prosperity. This pillar tracks violent crime rates, homicide, terrorism risk, political stability, armed conflict, police reliability, and property rights protection. High-scoring countries offer their citizens the freedom to live, work, and travel without fear — a basic human need that many still lack.
pillar.education.long_desc: Education unlocks human potential. This pillar evaluates literacy rates, school enrollment from primary to university, education spending, student-to-teacher ratios, PISA scores, and the quality gap between rich and poor students. Countries that invest wisely in education build a more skilled, innovative, and resilient workforce.
pillar.health.long_desc: A nation's health reveals its priorities. This pillar measures life expectancy, healthcare spending, hospital beds, doctors per capita, vaccination coverage, maternal and child mortality, and access to clean water and sanitation. Healthy populations are more productive, happier, and better prepared for future challenges.
pillar.environment.long_desc: The planet's health determines our future. This pillar tracks CO₂ emissions per capita, renewable energy share, air quality, forest coverage, biodiversity protection, water stress, and waste management. Countries that balance economic growth with environmental stewardship score highest — showing that prosperity and sustainability can coexist.
pillar.economy.long_desc: Economic opportunity means more than GDP. This pillar examines unemployment rates, youth employment, labor force participation, ease of doing business, foreign investment, trade openness, and economic freedom. High-scoring countries create environments where people can find meaningful work, start businesses, and improve their standard of living.
pillar.inclusion.long_desc: True prosperity leaves no one behind. This pillar measures gender equality, minority rights, religious tolerance, press freedom, civic participation, social mobility, and access to justice. Inclusive societies harness the talents of all their people — regardless of gender, ethnicity, religion, or economic background.
pillar.infrastructure.long_desc: Modern infrastructure connects people to opportunity. This pillar evaluates road quality, rail networks, port efficiency, air transport connectivity, internet access, mobile coverage, and electricity reliability. Countries with strong infrastructure enable faster trade, better services, and more equal access to economic participation.
pillar.innovation.long_desc: Innovation drives long-term prosperity. This pillar tracks R&D spending, patent applications, scientific publications, university-industry collaboration, tech startup ecosystems, and digital adoption. Countries that foster creativity and invest in research create the technologies, products, and solutions that shape tomorrow's economy.
pillar.equity.long_desc: Economic equity measures how fairly wealth is shared. This pillar examines the Gini coefficient, poverty rates, income share of the bottom 40%, social safety nets, minimum wage adequacy, and wealth concentration. Countries with greater equity have stronger consumer demand, less social tension, and more sustainable growth.
pillar.energy.long_desc: Energy powers everything — from homes to hospitals to factories. This pillar measures energy access, renewable energy share, energy efficiency, resource management, carbon intensity, and energy security. Countries transitioning to clean, reliable energy while ensuring universal access demonstrate that development and sustainability go hand in hand.
pillar.housing.long_desc: Affordable, quality housing is essential for dignity and stability. This pillar evaluates housing costs relative to income, homeownership rates, overcrowding, urban planning quality, slum populations, construction standards, and access to basic services. Countries that prioritize livable cities and affordable homes create communities where families can thrive.

about.title: About the Project
about.intro: The World Barometer (WB) is an open-data civic platform that monitors and visualizes prosperity indicators across countries worldwide.
about.mission.title: Our Mission
about.mission.text: To make government performance and societal progress transparent, comparable, and accessible to everyone through open data and clear visualization.
about.pillars.title: The 12 Pillars
about.pillars.text: We measure prosperity across 12 interconnected dimensions, from governance quality to housing affordability. Each pillar is scored 0–100 based on publicly available data from international organizations.
about.open.title: Open Data
about.open.text: All data comes from publicly accessible sources including the World Bank, United Nations, Freedom House, V-Dem Institute, and others. We do not collect any personal data from visitors.
about.contact.title: Contact
about.contact.text: Questions or feedback? Reach us at
methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.scoring.title: Scoring System
methodology.scoring.text: Each pillar is scored on a 0–100 scale, where 100 represents the best observed performance globally. Scores are derived from multiple sub-indicators normalized to this scale.
methodology.sources.title: Data Sources
methodology.sources.text: We aggregate data from the following international organizations and databases:
methodology.update.title: Update Frequency
methodology.update.text: Scores are updated as new data becomes available from source organizations, typically on an annual basis. The platform reflects the most recent available data.
methodology.limitations.title: Limitations
methodology.limitations.text: No single index can fully capture the complexity of national prosperity. Our scores should be viewed as approximate indicators based on available data, not definitive rankings.
impressum.title: Legal Notice
impressum.provider.name: WB World Initiative
impressum.responsible.name: Civic Data Team
impressum.liability.title: Liability for Content
impressum.liability.text: All data comes from publicly accessible sources (World Bank, Freedom House, UN, V-Dem). The platform has no political agenda.
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.highlight: We collect NO personal data. No cookies. No tracking.

privacy.analytics.title: Analytics
privacy.analytics.text: We use GoatCounter for anonymous statistics:
privacy.hosting.title: 2. Hosting
privacy.hosting.text: GitHub Pages — GitHub stores server logs for max 90 days (security).
privacy.sources.title: 3. External Sources
privacy.rights.title: 4. Your Rights
privacy.rights.text: Since we collect no personal data, there is nothing to disclose, correct, or delete.
privacy.contact.title: 5. Contact
about.support.title: Support This Project
about.support.text: The World Barometer is free, open, and independent. If you find it useful, you can support its continued development.

trade.title: Trade & Production
trade.global_title.long_desc: International trade connects economies and shapes prosperity. This overview ranks 160 countries by trade openness, exports, imports, and trade balance. Countries that trade more tend to grow faster, innovate more, and offer their citizens more diverse goods at lower prices. The data reveals which nations are export powerhouses, which depend heavily on imports, and which top products drive their economies.

desc.satisfaction: Life satisfaction score from the World Happiness Report, measuring subjective wellbeing on a 0–10 scale.
desc.corruption: Corruption Perceptions Index ranking by Transparency International. Lower rank means less perceived corruption.
desc.rule_of_law: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index, measuring accountability, open government, and access to justice. Scale 0–1.
desc.press_freedom: Reporters Without Borders press freedom ranking. Lower rank means greater press freedom.
desc.gdp: Gross Domestic Product: total monetary value of all goods and services produced in a year.
desc.gdp_per_capita: GDP divided by total population, indicating average economic output per person.
desc.public_debt: Total government debt as a percentage of GDP. Higher values indicate greater debt burden.
desc.unemployment: Percentage of the labor force that is jobless and actively seeking employment.
desc.inflation: Annual rate of increase in consumer prices. High inflation erodes purchasing power.
desc.gni_per_capita: Gross National Income per capita: total income earned by residents, including income from abroad.
desc.trade_exports: Total value of goods and services sold to other countries, in billions USD.
desc.trade_imports: Total value of goods and services purchased from other countries, in billions USD.
desc.trade_balance: Difference between exports and imports. Positive value means trade surplus, negative means deficit.
desc.trade_openness: Exports as a percentage of GDP, indicating how integrated the economy is with world trade.
desc.rev.taxes: Government income from direct taxes (income, corporate) and indirect taxes (VAT, excise).
desc.rev.social_contributions: Payments by employers and employees toward social insurance programs (pensions, healthcare).
desc.rev.grants: Foreign aid and transfers from international organizations or other governments.
desc.rev.other: Other government revenue sources including fees, fines, and state-owned enterprise income.
desc.exp.social_protection: Spending on pensions, unemployment benefits, disability support, and social welfare programs.
desc.exp.health: Government expenditure on public healthcare, hospitals, and health programs.
desc.exp.education: Public spending on education at all levels, from primary to university.
desc.exp.defense: Military and national defense spending, including personnel, equipment, and operations.
desc.exp.infrastructure: Spending on roads, bridges, public transport, water systems, and utilities.
desc.exp.public_services: General government administration, police, courts, and public order.
desc.exp.debt_service: Interest and principal payments on government debt obligations.
desc.exp.other: Other government expenditures not classified in the above categories.
summary.country.ranked: Ranked #{0} out of {1} countries with an overall score of {2}/100.
summary.country.score_label.average: This country performs at a global average level.
summary.country.score_label.below_avg: This country performs below the global average.
summary.country.score_label.low: This country faces significant development challenges.
summary.country.regime_info: Classified as a {0} with a democracy score of {1}/10.
summary.country.econ_highlight: GDP per capita is {0}, with {1}% unemployment and {2}% inflation.
summary.world.para1: How prosperous is our world, really? The Global Prosperity Barometer tracks {0} nations across 12 pillars — from governance and innovation to health and equity — to answer that question with data, not assumptions.
summary.world.para2: The picture that emerges is one of striking contrasts. {0} nations enjoy full democracy, {1} function as flawed democracies, {2} operate under hybrid regimes, and {3} remain under authoritarian rule. {4} countries live in peace, while {5} still face conflict or instability.
summary.world.para3: The economic divide is equally telling: average GDP per capita stands at just {0}, unemployment runs at {1}%, and inflation averages {2}% — numbers that mask enormous variation from country to country.
summary.world.para4: Where does the world excel? {0} lead the global rankings. But {1} lag behind — revealing where the greatest opportunities for progress lie.
summary.world.cta: Every nation has a story. Explore the data, find your country, and see where the world stands today.
desc.global_gdp: Total GDP of all tracked countries combined, measured in current US dollars.
desc.global_gdp_capita: Average GDP per person across all tracked countries.
desc.global_inflation: Average annual consumer price increase across all tracked countries.
desc.global_unemployment: Average share of the labor force without jobs across all tracked countries.
desc.global_debt: Average government debt as a percentage of GDP across all tracked countries.
demo.title: Demographics
desc.population: Total number of people living in the country, based on the latest census or UN estimate.
desc.pop_growth: Annual percentage change in population. Negative values indicate population decline.
desc.median_age: The age that divides the population into two equal halves. Higher median age indicates an aging society.
desc.life_exp: Average number of years a newborn is expected to live, based on current mortality rates.
desc.literacy: Percentage of people aged 15 and above who can read and write. Based on UNESCO data.
desc.density: Average number of people per square kilometer of land area.
desc.fertility: Average number of children born per woman over her lifetime. A rate of 2.1 is considered replacement level.
desc.infant_mortality: Number of deaths of infants under one year old per 1,000 live births.
